meta.setting: Saw camps, skid roads, bunkhouses, timber claims, and French Gulch freight yards
meta.rdr_clone_target: RDR-clone western with labor contracts, resource extraction, road control, injury, debt, and hard choices

research_grounding.title: NPS Whiskeytown History and Culture
research_grounding.use: Regional logging, ranching, mining, settlement, and resource-use context.

research_grounding.title: California State Parks Shasta State Historic Park
research_grounding.use: Northern California commerce, road supply, and mining-town material context.

generation_prompt.task: Generate the Timber Bosses cast page for the 1899 Jefferson State western packet. This JSON contains shape and guidance only.
page_shape: square image reference immediately after the title
opening blockquote with invented saw-camp note, stump warning, or contract saying generated later
H2 archetype subtitle in the register of Saw Camp Foremen
one or two compact lore paragraphs introducing logging foremen and timber owners controlling saw crews, roads, wages, and mountain scars
H3 Role with a short table-facing function phrase
H3 Traits with two to four terse signs or habits
10 H4 headings, each followed later by a brief playable move, leverage, cost, edge, or complication
H3 Camp Say closing blockquote with plain meaning generated later
generation_guidance: Generate timber bosses around saw camps, logging roads, crew danger, contracts, injury cover-up, road building, and resource conflict.
Tie future moves to work stoppage, timber theft, slope accidents, bunkhouse discipline, pay dock, hidden bodies, town bargaining, and choosing whether a road stays open.
Give them splinters and account books, not cartoon greed.
Let the page show how timber, mining, ranching, and Native land pressure overlap on the same roads.
Do not include final move names or final move text in this JSON.
generation_prompt.hand_cut_note: A timber boss should sound like a man who can measure a pine and a person in the same breath. The forest becomes money one felled tree at a time, and everybody hears it fall.
generation_prompt.table_spark: A timber-boss scene should ask who owns the road, who got hurt, who is paid to keep cutting, and what the hillside will look like afterward.
must_preserve: Use 1899 western logging and saw-camp language only.
Keep timber power practical, economic, and socially costly.
The generated page must include image path images/timber-bosses.jpg.
No finished page prose, final moves, or final sayings belong in this prompt file.
avoid: do not mention any prior packet, inherited taxonomy, or non-western source language
do not include medieval, enchanted, sacred, or fake-tribal framing
do not make logging quaint or consequence-free
do not use modern chainsaws, trucks, safety helmets, vehicles, or clean theme-park staging
self_check: The future markdown follows the exact heading sequence.
The future page is about saw camps, contracts, road control, injury, resource pressure, and table action.
The prompt contains no finished page prose.
All language belongs to grounded 1899 Jefferson State western play.
